chunks.principle: Smart course management is like being the casino instead of the gambler. Casinos always win because the odds favor them and they never get emotional. Stack the odds in your favor by making rational decisions on every shot.
chunks.application: Before each shot, ask yourself: 'Am I making this decision rationally, or am I reacting emotionally to my last shot?' If you're angry or anxious, that's a red flag to choose the conservative option.

chunks.principle: Start each hole thinking driver first, then find a good reason not to hit it. Tee shot distance is more important than accuracy for scoring — an extra 20 yards is worth more strokes to a 20-handicap than to a Tour pro.
chunks.application: Only lay back from driver when the landing area is dangerously narrow between serious trouble on both sides, or when there's a penalty area at your driver distance that you can't carry. Otherwise, hit driver.

chunks.principle: At every handicap level, double-bogey avoidance is a bigger scoring separator than birdies. One of the cornerstones of course management is making decisions that reduce double bogeys. It is much easier to prevent larger scores than to make a birdie.
chunks.application: After any mistake, shift your goal from 'save par' to 'save bogey.' Bogey is your friend. A round with 6 bogeys and no doubles (78) beats a round with 3 birdies and 4 doubles (81) every time.

chunks.principle: Your score on each hole is a lagging measure of the decisions you make on each shot. Instead of entering a round with a target score, commit to making the smartest decision possible on each individual shot.
chunks.application: Stop looking at the scorecard mid-round. Instead of 'I need to birdie the next hole to break 85,' think 'What's the smartest play here?' Focus on process, not results — the score will follow.

chunks.principle: Aim at the center of every green and play closer to the back yardage. Almost every golfer misses greens on the short side most of the time — taking more club fixes this instantly and leads to more greens in regulation.
chunks.application: The center of the green never moves. When in doubt, take one extra club and aim middle. You'll miss fewer greens short, have fewer short-sided chip shots, and your scores will drop without changing your swing.

chunks.principle: PGA Tour players make bogey 80% of the time in recovery situations. If the best golfers on earth can't save par from the trees, you certainly shouldn't expect to. The hero shot you see on TV is the exception, not the rule.
chunks.application: When your ball is in trouble — trees, deep rough, fairway bunker with a high lip — your only goal is getting back to safety. Punch sideways to the fairway, take your bogey, and move on. Two wrongs never make a right.

chunks.principle: Landing in light rough costs about 0.3 shots on average. Landing in trees costs 1.1 shots. Fairway bunkers cost 1.4 shots. Out of bounds is the worst penalty. These numbers should guide your target selection — light rough is not a disaster.
chunks.application: When trouble is on one side, aim your target toward the safe side, even if it means aiming into the rough. Missing the fairway into light rough is barely a penalty. Missing into trees or a bunker can ruin a hole.

chunks.principle: Pin hunting on par 3s is counterproductive. The combination of approach shot proximity and putting difficulty means that aiming at a tucked pin rarely produces birdies — it produces more short-sided misses and double bogeys.
chunks.application: On every par 3, aim center of green regardless of pin position. If you land on the green 25 feet from the hole, you're in great shape. A two-putt par is far better than the bogey or double that comes from chasing a tucked flag.

chunks.principle: Par 5s are where most golfers make their birdies, just like Tour pros. Tiger Woods made a disproportionate number of birdies on par 5s while playing conservatively on par 3s and 4s. Smart par-5 play means avoiding the bunkers.
chunks.application: On par 5s, identify the bunkers and hazards guarding the green. If you can't clear them with your second shot, lay up to a distance that takes them completely out of play — ideally your favorite wedge yardage (80-120 yards).

chunks.principle: Headwinds alter distance far more than tailwinds help. A 10 mph headwind adds about 10% of total distance plus 5 yards. A 10 mph tailwind only subtracts about 3% of distance. To go lower in wind, use more club — never swing harder.
chunks.application: In a headwind, add one club for every 5 mph of wind as a baseline, then adjust. For example, 150 yards into a 10 mph headwind plays about 165 yards. Take one or two more clubs and swing easy — hard swings add spin, which the wind magnifies.

chunks.principle: Think of birdies as an occasional bonus for playing smart golf, not as a goal. A typical PGA Tour player averages only 3.5 birdies per round, and most occur on par 5s. Recreational golfers seldom make birdies even at scratch level.
chunks.application: Never say 'I need to make a birdie here.' Instead, play for smart position and let birdies happen naturally when putts fall. Actively chasing birdies leads to aggressive decisions that create doubles.

chunks.principle: Every golfer has a two-way miss, no matter what shot shape they play. Even Dustin Johnson, ranked #1 in strokes gained off the tee, misses almost equally left and right. You cannot eliminate one side of the course.
chunks.application: Don't plan your tee shot assuming you'll only miss one direction. Your driver dispersion is probably 55-70 yards wide. Accept both sides of your pattern exist and choose targets that keep both misses out of serious trouble.

chunks.principle: Studying a course before you play with satellite imagery is one of the most effective ways to lower scores. Pre-round planning takes about 10-15 minutes and primarily focuses on tee shot club selection and targets based on hazard distances.
chunks.application: Before playing an unfamiliar course, pull up Google Earth and note: (1) where big trouble is on each tee shot, (2) distances to fairway bunkers and water, (3) which holes might need less than driver. Bring these notes to the course.

chunks.principle: When short-sided near a green, trying to land the ball right at the edge brings double bogey into play. The smart play is to land past the hole — you might leave a longer putt for par, but you virtually guarantee bogey at worst.
chunks.application: On a short-sided chip, shift your landing target past the hole. You'll putt from further away but on the putting surface. A 20-foot putt for par beats a 3-foot chip attempt that might not even reach the green.

chunks.principle: Knowing your real distances — not your best-ever, but your average carry with each club — is the foundation of course management. Most golfers overestimate their distances by 10-15 yards, leading to chronically short approach shots.
chunks.application: Use your actual average distance, not your 'when I really catch it' distance. If your 7-iron sometimes goes 160 but usually goes 145, play it as a 145-yard club. You'll miss fewer greens short, which is where most scoring damage occurs.
